turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've just been feeling really miserable for the past two weeks. I've had this persistent cough that's been getting worse and I've also been having trouble breathing - it feels like my lungs are working harder than usual.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: It's just so frustrating because I feel like I've tried everything to get rid of it - cough syrup, honey, steam inhalation... nothing seems to be helping. And to make matters worse, I've also had a low-grade fever that comes and goes, which is making me really tired all the time.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: When I'm having these breathing issues, I get this wheezing sound when I breathe out, it's like a high-pitched whistling noise. And sometimes, especially at night, I wake up feeling really short of breath and my heart is racing a bit.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: To be honest, the sweating doesn't seem to be too bad, but I have been having some pretty intense night sweats lately. As for feeling faint, yeah, sometimes when I'm really short of breath and my heart is racing, I feel a bit lightheaded, like I might pass out if I stand up too quickly.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It's more of a general feeling all over my body, but when I breathe deeply or cough, I feel this sharp pain on my right side, just under my ribs. It's hard to describe, but it feels like something is pinching me there.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah... I've also been feeling really fatigued and lethargic lately. Even just getting out of bed in the morning feels like a chore, and I've had to miss work a few days because I couldn't muster up the energy to go in. It's not just physical exhaustion, either - I feel mentally drained too, like I'm just going through the motions.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: To be honest, no... I haven't really noticed any swelling or puffiness anywhere on my body. But now that you mention it, I have been feeling a bit more congested in my head and sinuses lately, especially when I wake up in the morning. And, um, I think I should mention one other thing - I've been using cetirizine to help with my seasonal allergies, but even taking that hasn't really helped much with this congestion.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: Actually, yes... I've been feeling a bit queasy and my appetite has been off for the past few days. Even thinking about food makes me feel a bit nauseated, which is weird because I usually love cooking and trying new recipes. It's just been really hard to muster up any interest in eating lately.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: It's like my batteries are drained, you know? I have no energy to do anything, even simple tasks feel overwhelming. And with my appetite, it's not just that I'm not hungry, but even thinking about food makes me feel queasy and anxious. It's really frustrating because I love cooking and trying new recipes, but now it's just something I don't want to deal with.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've been having a low-grade fever, around 38.2 degrees Celsius, and it's been intermittent - sometimes it comes and goes throughout the day, other times it just lingers in the evenings. The night sweats have actually been pretty intense, I'll wake up drenched in sweat every night.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: Actually, my mom has asthma, and she's always been a bit of a worrier, so I'm probably getting a little anxious about this too! But aside from that, I don't have any other known medical conditions. I did used to get these wheezing episodes when I was younger, around age 8 or 9, but they never really got formally diagnosed. They'd happen whenever I had a cold or exercised too much, and they'd go away on their own after a few days. But now that you mention it, maybe that's something we should look into...

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been trying my best to take everything as prescribed, but it's been really tough because nothing seems to be working. I've even started using over-the-counter dextromethorphan for the cough, but like you said, it hasn't brought much relief. And to make matters worse, I'm also worried about taking too many medications and potentially interacting with each other.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: I try to eat pretty healthily, I guess. As an elementary school teacher, I'm usually always on-the-go and grabbing something quick for breakfast before work. Sometimes it's just cereal or toast with fruit, other times it's scrambled eggs or avocado toast if I've got a bit more time. Lunch is usually leftovers from last night's dinner or a sandwich from the café down the street. And in the evenings, my partner and I like to cook together - we make a pretty good team! We're both big fans of trying out new recipes, but like I said earlier, even thinking about food lately makes me feel queasy...

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: I try to stay active, but it's been tough lately. I usually take my dog for a walk in the evenings, and I've been trying to squeeze in some yoga at home when I have the energy. But even simple activities like that feel exhausting right now. And exercise-wise, I used to be pretty regular about going to the gym or taking Zumba classes, but since this cough started, I just haven't had the motivation or energy to do much of anything physical.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: To be honest, my mom has asthma, and she's always been a bit of a worrier, so I'm probably getting a little anxious about this too! But aside from that, I don't have any other known medical conditions. I did used to get these wheezing episodes when I was younger, around age 8 or 9, but they never really got formally diagnosed. They'd happen whenever I had a cold or exercised too much, and they'd go away on their own after a few days. But now that you mention it, maybe that's something we should look into...

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yeah... (pausing for a moment) To be honest, yeah. I do worry about my own health because of it. It's like, I know my mom has asthma and it's not something that's been passed down to me, but still... there's always this tiny voice in the back of my head wondering if maybe I'm more susceptible to respiratory issues or something because of our family history. And now with all these symptoms and no clear answers, it's hard not to get a little anxious about what might be going on inside my body.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I do drink socially, usually just 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends with my partner. As for coping with stress, I'm not really sure... lately, it's felt like everything is just piling up and I don't know how to deal with it all. I try to stay busy with work and other activities, but sometimes that just feels overwhelming too. To be honest, I've been feeling a bit lost on how to manage my stress levels lately.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke at all and I've never used any recreational drugs. And actually, just one more thing - I did have a bit of exposure to someone who was sick recently... my partner had a mild cold about a week ago, but he's been fully recovered since then. We're pretty careful about keeping our living space clean and germ-free, so I don't think that's the cause of my symptoms, but I wanted to mention it just in case.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: I think I may have left out some important details. One thing I didn't mention is that I've had a bit of exposure to my students at school, and several of them have been sick with upper respiratory infections in the past month. And also, I live with my partner and a cat, so maybe there's something going on with the environment or our pets?

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient:
I'm not sure if it's relevant, but I did have a bit of a scare when I was younger. My maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer, and sometimes I worry that maybe I inherited some kind of genetic predisposition for respiratory issues. I know it's probably not the most rational thing to think about, but it's hard not to wonder if there's some connection.

Also, I almost forgot to mention that I had a physical exam just over a year ago, and everything seemed normal at the time. But now that I'm thinking back on it, maybe that's something we should look into...
